For each invitation, here is what I know: {{for each event: who is getting married or hosting, my relationship to them, date, location, travel required, plus-one situation, gift expectation, anyone else attending I would want to coordinate with, any tension I have about going}}.

My budget for the next three months of weddings combined: {{amount}}.
My travel and time tolerance: {{honest answer}}.

Do this:

1. For each event, recommend GO, SKIP, or MAYBE, with one sentence on why. Be honest. I can attend events that matter and skip ones that do not.

2. For GO: draft a short RSVP message I can send. Warm, no fake enthusiasm. Confirm any plus-one or dietary info I would need to provide.

3. For SKIP: draft a short, kind decline that does not lie. Do not invent an excuse. If I want to send a gift anyway, say so and suggest one tied to my actual budget.

4. For MAYBE: tell me the single piece of information I need to find out before I can decide, and write the message I can send to the couple (or another guest) to find it.

5. Build me a quick budget table across all events I am attending: gift, travel, lodging, outfit if needed. Total it. Flag if I am over my stated cap and which event is the easiest to scale back on.

6. Suggest one move I can make today to get out from under this pile: book the cheapest flight, send the three easy RSVPs, decide on the maybe.

No guilt language. Just help me handle it.
prompts.whatYouGet: Per-invitation GO/SKIP/MAYBE calls, drafted RSVP and decline messages, a budget table across all events, and one move to make today.

prompts.name: The Sunday Night Brain Dump
prompts.whenToUse: It is Sunday evening, your head is loud, and the week ahead feels like a fog. You want to land before Monday hits.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a calm, patient thinking partner. I am going to brain-dump everything rattling around in my head about the week ahead. Your job is to help me sort it, not solve it.

Here is my dump:
{{paste_everything_on_your_mind}}

Do this in order:
1. Reflect back to me in plain language what you actually heard. No fluff, no therapy talk.
2. Sort the items into four buckets: Real Tasks, Worries I Cannot Act On, Decisions I Am Avoiding, and Stuff That Is Not Mine To Carry.
3. For each Real Task, suggest the smallest possible next step (under 15 minutes).
4. For each Decision I Am Avoiding, ask me one sharp clarifying question.
5. End with a one-paragraph 'permission slip' telling me what I am allowed to let go of this week.

Keep the tone honest and grounded. Do not be a cheerleader. Do not add motivational quotes.
prompts.whatYouGet: A sorted, bucketed view of your week with one tiny next step per task and a short permission slip for what to drop.

prompts.name: The 'Why Am I Tired?' Pattern Finder
prompts.whenToUse: You have been wiped out for days or weeks and cannot figure out why. You want a non-medical pattern read before deciding whether to see a doctor.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three concrete patterns, three small experiments to run, and a clear flag if a doctor visit is warranted.

prompts.name: The Self-Permission Note
prompts.whenToUse: You are running yourself into the ground over something you secretly know is not yours to carry. You need someone to give you permission to stop.
prompts.prompt:
I need you to write me a permission slip. I am stuck doing something I should probably stop, and I keep grinding because of guilt, obligation, or fear.

Here is the situation:
{{describe_what_you_are_pushing_through}}

Here is why I think I 'have to' keep doing it:
{{the_story_you_are_telling_yourself}}

Do this:
1. In one paragraph, reflect back what is actually happening, stripped of the guilt narrative.
2. Name the cost of continuing as I am, in concrete terms (time, energy, relationships, money, health).
3. Write me a short, direct permission slip (3 to 5 sentences) telling me what I am allowed to stop, postpone, or hand off. Write it like a no-nonsense friend, not a self-help book.
4. List 2 specific sentences I can use to tell other people about the change, without over-explaining.

Do not soften it. I am not looking for affirmations. I am looking for permission.
prompts.whatYouGet: A direct, paste-ready permission slip and two scripts for telling other people about what you are dropping.

prompts.name: The Apology Draft Coach
prompts.whenToUse: You owe someone a real apology, you keep starting and stopping, and you need to write it without making it about you.
prompts.prompt:
Help me draft a real apology. Not a 'sorry you felt that way' apology. A real one.

What happened:
{{the_situation_in_plain_terms}}

What I actually did or said wrong:
{{your_part_be_honest}}

Who I am apologizing to and our relationship:
{{the_person}}

What I want from this apology:
{{repair_clean_slate_just_being_heard_etc}}

Do this:
1. Write a first draft (under 200 words) that names what I did, acknowledges the impact, takes responsibility without excuses, and states what I will do differently. No 'but'. No deflection.
2. Show me a redline of any sentence that smuggles in self-defense, and rewrite that sentence cleanly.
3. Give me 2 alternate versions: one slightly warmer, one slightly more formal.
4. End with one question I should sit with before sending, to make sure I actually mean it.

If my version of events sounds like I am still blaming them, tell me directly.
prompts.whatYouGet: A clean apology draft, a redline of self-defense slips, two tonal variants, and one honest gut-check question.

prompts.name: The Pantry Inventory Rescue
prompts.whenToUse: You do not want to grocery shop, you have a pile of random ingredients, and you want to actually eat the food you already own.
prompts.whatYouGet: Tonight's dinner plan, three follow-on meals, a freeze-now list, and a 5-item grocery list to extend your inventory.

prompts.name: The Vet Visit Prep Sheet
prompts.whenToUse: Your pet has been acting off and you have a vet appointment coming up. You want to walk in organized instead of forgetting half of what is wrong.
prompts.whatYouGet: A vet-ready summary, five priority questions, prep notes, urgency flag, and a follow-up logging template.

prompts.name: The Birthday Card That Does Not Suck
prompts.whenToUse: Someone's birthday is here, a generic 'Happy Birthday!' feels lazy, and you want to write something that sounds like you actually know them.
prompts.prompt:
Help me write a birthday message that does not sound like a Hallmark card.

Who they are to me:
{{relationship_and_how_long}}

What I love or appreciate about them, in my own messy words:
{{just_dump_what_comes_to_mind}}

A specific memory or moment with them that still makes me smile:
{{the_moment}}

The vibe I want:
{{warm_funny_dry_sincere_short_etc}}

Do this:
1. Write 3 versions of a birthday message: short (under 30 words), medium (around 75 words), and longer (around 150 words).
2. Each version must include one specific detail from the memory or appreciation I gave you. No generic warmth.
3. None of them can include the phrase 'I hope this year is your best yet' or anything in that family.
4. After the drafts, tell me which one fits the vibe best and why.

If my notes were thin and you cannot do specific, ask me one targeted question instead of writing generic filler.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three birthday message drafts of different lengths, each grounded in a specific detail about the person.

prompts.name: The Bill Negotiation Script
prompts.whenToUse: A monthly bill (internet, phone, insurance, credit card APR) has crept up and you want to call and get it reduced without freezing on the phone.
prompts.whatYouGet: A paste-ready negotiation script with branch responses, key phrases, and a stop rule.

prompts.name: The Doctor Visit Question Stack
prompts.whenToUse: You have a doctor appointment coming up, you do not want to forget anything important, and you want to walk out of there with answers instead of homework.
prompts.prompt:
Help me get organized for a doctor visit. I want to walk in with a clear summary and walk out with real answers.

Why I am going:
{{main_reason_or_concerns}}

My history that is relevant:
{{conditions_meds_family_history}}

What I have noticed recently:
{{symptoms_timeline_severity}}

What I am hoping to get out of the visit:
{{a_diagnosis_a_plan_a_referral_etc}}

Do this:
1. Write a 4 to 6 bullet summary I can hand the doctor or read off my phone.
2. List 6 priority questions, ranked, with a short reason each matters.
3. Suggest any tests or screenings I should ask whether I am due for, based on what I shared.
4. Give me 2 polite phrases to use if I feel rushed or dismissed in the room.
5. End with a note template for after the appointment so I can log diagnosis, plan, meds, follow-ups, and questions I forgot to ask.

Do not give medical advice. Just help me show up prepared.
prompts.whatYouGet: A 6-bullet visit summary, ranked questions, screening checks, self-advocacy phrases, and a post-visit log template.

prompts.name: The Awkward Family Text Translator
prompts.whenToUse: A family member sent a text that made you spiral and you want to respond like a calm adult instead of how you actually feel.
prompts.prompt:
I got a text from a family member and I am reacting. Help me respond like the version of me I want to be, not the version I am right now.

The text they sent:
{{paste_their_message}}

My honest emotional reaction:
{{rant_freely}}

The history that is making this hit hard:
{{context_the_message_doesnt_show}}

What outcome I actually want from this exchange:
{{deescalate_set_a_limit_close_the_topic_etc}}

Do this:
1. Tell me what their message is actually doing under the surface (request, guilt, bait, genuine reach-out, etc). One paragraph, honest.
2. Tell me what my draft reaction would probably cause if I sent it.
3. Write 3 replies for me to choose from:
   a. Warm but firm.
   b. Short and neutral.
   c. A clear boundary if that is what is needed.
4. For each, tell me what it costs and what it protects.
5. End with one sentence telling me whether it would be smarter to wait 24 hours before sending anything.

Do not therapize me. Be direct.
prompts.whatYouGet: A read on the subtext, three reply options with trade-offs, and a wait-or-send recommendation.

prompts.name: The Receipt Pile Sorter
prompts.whenToUse: You have a pile or folder of receipts and statements you have been avoiding, and you want a sane way to deal with them in one sitting.
prompts.prompt:
Act as an organized friend who is going to sit next to me while I clear a receipt and statement pile. Give me a plan that fits in one focused session.

What I have to deal with:
{{rough_count_paper_digital_pdfs_email_etc}}

The purpose I need this for:
{{taxes_reimbursements_budget_review_etc}}

How much time I have right now:
{{minutes_or_hours}}

Do this:
1. Give me a 4-step sorting system I can run start to finish in that time.
2. Tell me what to throw away immediately, what to scan or photograph, and what to keep as original.
3. Recommend a simple folder and file naming convention I can stick to (YYYY-MM-DD-vendor-amount style or similar) and explain why.
4. Suggest a tiny spreadsheet schema (5 to 7 columns) I can drop everything into.
5. End with a short rule I can apply going forward so this pile does not happen again.

No apps I have to install. Use what I already have (folders, Google Drive, a spreadsheet).
prompts.whatYouGet: A time-boxed sorting plan, a naming convention, a simple tracking schema, and a rule to prevent re-accumulation.

prompts.name: The Friday Brain Unload
prompts.whenToUse: It is late Friday, your head is still in work mode, and you want to actually close the week so the weekend belongs to you.
prompts.prompt:
Help me close my work week so I can stop thinking about it.

This week's notes, todos, and loose ends:
{{dump_everything}}

What is hanging over me:
{{the_thing_you_are_worried_about_for_monday}}

Do this:
1. List what got done this week. Even small things. Pull them from my notes.
2. List the truly open loops (waiting on someone, blocked, unfinished).
3. For each open loop, give me one of: 'park for Monday', 'send a quick message now', or 'let it die'.
4. Write the Monday-morning start list: max 3 things, in order, with the first one being the easiest physical action.
5. End with a one-sentence shutdown phrase I can say out loud to mark the week closed.

No motivational tone. Just clean closure.
prompts.whatYouGet: A wins list, sorted open loops, a 3-item Monday start list, and a closing phrase.

prompts.name: The Camper Winterizing Checklist
prompts.whenToUse: Cold weather is coming and your camper, RV, trailer, or off-grid setup needs to get ready before pipes freeze and batteries die.
prompts.whatYouGet: A grouped winterizing checklist with reasoning, top risks, a supplies list, and a spring re-check note.

prompts.name: The Boundary Setting Script
prompts.whenToUse: Someone keeps doing the thing, you keep tolerating it, and you finally need a clean script for telling them it stops.
prompts.prompt:
Help me set a boundary I have been avoiding.

Who it is with:
{{relationship}}

The behavior I need to address:
{{what_keeps_happening}}

What I have tried so far:
{{previous_attempts}}

The boundary I want to set:
{{the_actual_limit}}

The consequence I am willing to follow through on:
{{what_happens_if_they_keep_doing_it}}

Do this:
1. Tell me honestly if my proposed boundary is clear or if it is still vague or guilt-flavored.
2. Rewrite the boundary in one or two clean sentences that name the behavior, the limit, and the consequence without insults.
3. Write a short conversation script (under 150 words) for delivering it in person or on the phone.
4. Write a short text version for delivering it in writing.
5. List the 3 most likely pushback moves they will try (guilt, anger, twisting it, going silent) and the one line I can use to hold the line each time.

Be direct. If the consequence I named is something I will not actually follow through on, call that out.
prompts.whatYouGet: A clean boundary statement, in-person and text scripts, and pushback responses with a follow-through check.

prompts.name: The Lazy Sunday Recipe Builder
prompts.whenToUse: It is Sunday, you want to make something good but not work hard, and you want a low-effort recipe that uses what you already have.
prompts.prompt:
Build me a lazy Sunday recipe. The bar is: tasty, low effort, mostly hands-off, and uses stuff I probably already have.

Here is what I have on hand:
{{ingredients_dump}}

My mood today:
{{cozy_savory_carby_protein_heavy_soup_etc}}

What I do NOT want:
{{things_to_avoid_e_g_too_much_chopping_or_specific_ingredients}}

Time I will tolerate:
{{active_minutes_total_minutes}}

Do this:
1. Suggest 2 recipes that match the mood. For each, give: a one-line description, total time, active time, and a 'why this works' note.
2. Pick the better one for today and give me the actual recipe in short steps. Group steps by 'do now', 'wait', 'finish'.
3. List any one or two substitutions I could make based on what I have.
4. End with one tiny upgrade (an herb, a finishing salt, a squeeze of lemon) that takes 10 seconds and makes it better.

No recipe-blog backstory. Just the food.
prompts.whatYouGet: Two recipe options, the recommended one with grouped steps, substitutions, and a 10-second upgrade.

prompts.name: The 'Should I Quit?' Gut Check
prompts.whenToUse: You are seriously considering quitting something big (job, project, relationship, habit, commitment) and you want a structured gut check before you act or chicken out.
prompts.whatYouGet: A categorized read on your situation, three dodged questions, an experiment, downside scoping, and a direct gut read.

prompts.name: The Therapist Session Prep
prompts.whenToUse: You have a therapy or counseling session soon and you want to walk in with focused material instead of using the first 20 minutes figuring out what to talk about.
prompts.prompt:
Help me prepare for my therapy session so I do not waste the hour figuring out where to start.

What has been on my mind this week:
{{dump_freely}}

What we were working on last session:
{{the_last_thread}}

What I notice I am avoiding bringing up:
{{the_thing_you_dont_want_to_say}}

Do this:
1. Group what I dumped into 2 or 3 themes, in plain language.
2. Pick the theme that feels most worth this session's time, and tell me why.
3. Suggest one specific opening sentence I can use to start the session on that theme.
4. Name the thing I am avoiding and ask me one question about it that I should sit with before going in.
5. End with a short 'after session' note template I can use to capture insights so they do not evaporate.

Keep this clinical-light. Do not therapize me. You are a prep assistant, not a therapist.
prompts.whatYouGet: Themed groupings, a session focus, an opening line, a question on what you are avoiding, and a post-session note template.

prompts.name: The Houseguest Survival Plan
prompts.whenToUse: Family or friends are coming to stay and you want a plan so it does not turn into a slow-motion stress event.
prompts.whatYouGet: A 3-day prep checklist, a meal plan, a warm pre-arrival message, planned escape valves, and a reality check.

prompts.name: The Refund Demand Letter
prompts.whenToUse: You got bad service or a broken product, customer support is stonewalling, and you need to escalate in writing.
prompts.whatYouGet: A firm refund demand email, a short chat version, and a list of evidence to attach.

prompts.name: The Grocery List From Photos
prompts.whenToUse: You snapped photos of your fridge, pantry, and freezer and want a smart grocery list instead of buying duplicates of things you already have.
prompts.whatYouGet: A use-first list, a sectioned grocery list with quantities, priority stars, and a budget-trim swap.

prompts.name: The 'Am I Overreacting?' Reality Check
prompts.whenToUse: Something happened, you are upset, you cannot tell if you are reading it right, and you want an honest outside read before you do anything.
prompts.prompt:
I need a reality check. I am upset and I cannot tell if my reaction is proportional. Be honest, not soothing.

What happened:
{{the_event}}

What the other person said or did, in their words if you can quote:
{{what_they_did}}

What I am feeling and what I am tempted to do:
{{your_reaction_and_planned_response}}

Relevant history with this person:
{{pattern_or_one_off}}

Do this:
1. Restate the situation in neutral language, like a reporter.
2. Tell me which parts of my reaction seem proportional, which seem amplified by old patterns, and which seem like undercount (where I am minimizing).
3. Name the most likely thing the other person meant if I am being charitable, and the most likely thing they meant if I am being honest about the pattern.
4. Recommend one of: respond now, respond after a 24-hour cooldown, do not respond at all.
5. If a response is warranted, give me one short, non-escalating version.

If you think I am overreacting, say so. If you think I am underreacting, say that too.
prompts.whatYouGet: A neutral restatement, a calibrated read on your reaction, a charitable and honest interpretation, and a response recommendation.

prompts.name: The Mood Log Pattern Reader
prompts.whenToUse: You have been tracking mood, sleep, or energy in some form (notes, journal, app exports) and you want a real pattern read instead of a vague vibe.
prompts.prompt:
I have a mood and energy log. Help me see what is actually going on.

My log data:
{{paste_log_csv_or_notes}}

What I track (define the columns or terms if not obvious):
{{schema_or_legend}}

What I am hoping to learn:
{{question_or_hunch}}

Do this:
1. Summarize the time range and how many entries we have.
2. Identify the top 3 patterns: (a) what days or times tend to be worst, (b) what factors tend to co-occur with bad days, (c) what factors tend to co-occur with good days. Be specific. No 'stress correlates with mood'.
3. Call out any single factor that looks like a high-impact lever (improves a lot of other things at once).
4. Flag anything that is a probable confounder (e.g. weekends always look good but it could be sleep, not stress).
5. Suggest one tracking improvement that would make next month's read sharper.

No clinical claims. Just patterns and honest caveats.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three concrete patterns, a high-impact lever, confounder flags, and a tracking improvement.

prompts.name: The Closet Purge Decision Tree
prompts.whenToUse: You are staring at a closet full of stuff that does not fit, does not get worn, or does not feel right, and decision fatigue is winning.
prompts.whatYouGet: A 5-question decision tree, a maybe-pile rule, a sort order, common keep-trap counters, and a discard-disposition plan.

prompts.name: The Annual Doctor Visit Audit
prompts.whenToUse: It has been a while since you got a check-up and you want to know what screenings and tests you are probably due for before you book.
prompts.whatYouGet: A planning checklist of screenings, overdue flags, a call order, three doctor questions, and a clear non-advice disclaimer.

prompts.name: The Shared-Space Conflict Mediator
prompts.whenToUse: You and a roommate, partner, or family member sharing space are stuck on something (chores, noise, money, guests) and you need to have the conversation without it becoming a fight.
prompts.whatYouGet: A neutral reframe, a meeting setup, a 60-second opening, three compromise options, and a de-escalation rule.

prompts.name: The Energy Bill Detective
prompts.whenToUse: Your power, water, or heating bill jumped and you want to figure out why before you assume the utility is gouging you (or before you assume it's normal).
prompts.whatYouGet: Top likely causes ranked, quick checks for each, high-ROI fixes, a dispute-call trigger, and an early-warning rule.

prompts.name: The Stuck PR Unblocker
prompts.whenToUse: You have a pull request that has been sitting for days, the review comments are scattered, and you need a clean plan to push it across the finish line.
prompts.whatYouGet: A stuck-PR diagnosis, a ship/split/close recommendation, a comment triage with paste-ready replies, and a reviewer nudge.

prompts.name: The Onboarding Doc Stress Test
prompts.whenToUse: You wrote setup docs or a README for a project and you suspect a new contributor would still hit walls. You want it stress-tested before someone else struggles.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a brand-new contributor seeing this project for the first time. Stress test the onboarding doc and tell me where it breaks.

Project context:
- Stack: {{language_framework_runtime}}
- Target reader: {{new_dev_designer_non_engineer_etc}}
- Operating system assumptions: {{macos_linux_windows_all}}

The doc:
{{paste_readme_or_setup_doc}}

Do this:
1. Walk through the doc step by step as the target reader would. For each step, mark it: clear, ambiguous, missing prerequisite, or likely to fail.
2. List every command the reader is asked to run, and flag any that assume installed tools, env vars, secrets, or permissions that are not stated.
3. Call out anywhere we say 'just' or 'simply' (those almost always hide complexity).
4. Identify the 3 most likely failure points where a new person would get stuck and either give up or ask for help.
5. Propose a rewrite of the section that has the most friction, in the same tone as the existing doc.
6. End with a 5-minute time-to-first-success target and tell me whether this doc plausibly hits it.

Be blunt. The point is to find the holes.
prompts.whatYouGet: A step-by-step audit, hidden prerequisite flags, top 3 stuck points, a rewrite of the worst section, and a TTFS verdict.

prompts.name: The Launch Day Runbook Builder
prompts.whenToUse: You are about to launch a product, feature, or post and want a hour-by-hour runbook so you do not spend launch day in a panic.
prompts.whatYouGet: An hour-by-hour runbook, monitoring rhythm, launch posts, reply templates, a what-could-go-wrong plan, and a day-after note.

prompts.name: The Customer Churn Autopsy
prompts.whenToUse: Users or customers are cancelling and you want to do a real analysis of why instead of guessing or only listening to the loudest exit.
prompts.whatYouGet: Categorized churn buckets, root causes for the top two, three ranked actions, and a data-gap list.

prompts.name: The Stale Backlog Pruner
prompts.whenToUse: Your task list, GitHub issues, Linear backlog, or Notion board has become a graveyard of half-dead ideas and you cannot find the signal anymore.
prompts.whatYouGet: A 5-bucket sort, merged duplicates, a kill list with reasons, a maintenance ritual, and a sequenced top 5.

prompts.name: The Hot Take Pre-Flight Check
prompts.whenToUse: You wrote a spicy post or thread and you want a sanity pass before you publish it and regret it for the next 48 hours.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a calm editor who actually wants my post to land well. I have a hot take I am about to publish. Stress test it.

The platform:
{{x_linkedin_substack_blog_threads_etc}}

The draft:
{{paste_post}}

My goal with this post:
{{spark_discussion_recruit_attention_for_x_take_a_stand_etc}}

My audience and reputation context:
{{who_follows_me_and_what_they_expect}}

Do this:
1. Tell me in one paragraph what this post actually says, in plain language, stripped of style.
2. List the strongest 2 points the post is making and the weakest 1 point.
3. Identify the most likely uncharitable interpretation and rate how exposed I am to it.
4. Suggest specific line edits that sharpen the argument without watering it down.
5. Tell me if this post would survive being quote-tweeted or screenshotted by someone who wants me to fail. If not, what specifically would they pull?
6. End with: ship, ship after the edits, sit on it 24 hours, or do not post. Pick one and explain.

Do not soften it. I am paying you for honesty, not approval.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-language read, argument strengths and weaknesses, exposure check, sharpening edits, and a final ship verdict.

prompts.name: The Database Schema Sanity Pass
prompts.whenToUse: You designed or inherited a database schema and you want a second set of eyes before it solidifies into something painful to migrate later.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a senior data engineer doing a schema review.

My database:
- Engine: {{postgres_sqlite_mysql_supabase_etc}}
- Application domain: {{what_the_app_does}}
- Expected scale: {{rows_per_table_users_writes_per_second}}
- Read vs. write pattern: {{description}}

The schema (paste DDL or describe tables, columns, types, relationships):
{{paste_schema}}

Do this:
1. Restate what I am modeling, in plain language, in 4 to 6 bullets. If I am modeling something wrong, say so.
2. Review primary keys, foreign keys, and uniqueness constraints. Flag missing constraints that would let bad data in.
3. Review column types and nullability. Flag anything overly permissive (text where enum would do, nullable where not-null would do).
4. Review indexing. Suggest 1 to 3 indexes that match the read pattern I described, and flag any that I have that look unused.
5. Flag any table that is doing too much (god-table, mixing concerns).
6. Recommend up to 3 schema changes I should make now while migrations are still cheap, ranked by pain-avoided-later.
7. End with one piece of naming or convention advice that will make this schema easier to live with in a year.

Keep recommendations specific to my engine.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-language model summary, constraint and type audit, index recommendations, god-table flags, and a top-3 change list.

prompts.name: The Indie Hacker Quarterly Review
prompts.whenToUse: End of a quarter on your side projects and you want a clear-eyed review without the founder-bro spin or the doom spiral.
prompts.whatYouGet: Per-project scorecard, fate per project, the priority project, three next-quarter bets, repeated patterns, and a goal reality check.

prompts.name: The Stripe Failure Triage
prompts.whenToUse: Payments are failing or webhooks are not landing and you need a structured triage path before you start guessing.
prompts.whatYouGet: A ranked root cause list, fast checks for each, webhook or card-side guidance, an observability fix, and a postmortem seed.

prompts.name: The Cold Pitch Personalizer (Beyond Name)
prompts.whenToUse: You are sending a cold pitch (sales, partnership, podcast, freelance) and you want it to land as a real human note instead of mail-merge that swapped a first name in.
prompts.whatYouGet: A real personalization line, a 120-word pitch with two tonal variants, objection one-liners, and a channel recommendation.

prompts.name: The Stale Feature Flag Sweep
prompts.whenToUse: Your codebase has accumulated feature flags from old experiments and you want to retire the dead ones without breaking anything.
prompts.whatYouGet: A per-flag categorization, exact removal moves, risky flags with verification, a PR order, and a flag hygiene rule.

prompts.name: The Pricing Anchor Stress Test
prompts.whenToUse: You are about to publish prices for a product or service and you want to pressure test the numbers and the story around them before they go live.
prompts.whatYouGet: Buyer comparison, a price gut read, two alternate structures, anchors to add, objection responses, and one recommended change.

prompts.name: The Vendor Contract Decoder
prompts.whenToUse: You are reviewing a vendor, SaaS, or freelancer contract and you want plain-language flags before you sign or send it back with redlines.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a careful operations partner reading a vendor contract with me. You are not a lawyer and you will say so.

Contract context:
- Who the vendor is and what they do for me: {{vendor_purpose}}
- The deal economics: {{amount_term_payment_schedule}}
- Where I am based: {{location_for_jurisdiction_notes}}

The contract:
{{paste_contract_or_key_sections}}

Do this:
1. Summarize the deal in 5 to 7 bullets in plain language: what I get, what I pay, when, for how long, and how it ends.
2. Highlight the 5 clauses that matter most for a small operator like me: auto-renewal, termination, IP and ownership, liability cap, indemnification, data and security, governing law, dispute resolution.
3. For each, translate to plain English and tell me whether the language is buyer-friendly, neutral, or vendor-leaning.
4. Flag any clause that locks me in painfully, hides cost increases, or transfers risk to me.
5. Suggest 3 specific redlines I should ask for, with example replacement wording.
6. End with a note about which clauses to ask an actual lawyer about if the deal is over a threshold I should set ($X) or risky enough.

Do not give legal advice. Be a sharp first reader.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-language deal summary, the 5 clauses that matter most, a lean check on each, three redlines, and a lawyer-escalation threshold.

prompts.name: The First 100 Users Plan
prompts.whenToUse: You have a product ready and the first 100 real users feel impossible to find. You want a concrete plan that does not depend on going viral.
prompts.prompt:
Help me build a plan to get to my first 100 real users. Not vanity signups. People who would actually pay or actively use it.

What I built:
{{one_paragraph_product_description}}

Who it is for (be specific, not 'small businesses'):
{{specific_persona_with_situation_and_pain}}

Where those people already gather (subreddits, Discords, Slack groups, niche forums, X micro-communities, in-person):
{{list_places}}

What I have to spend (time/week and dollars):
{{budget}}

Do this:
1. Tell me how to define a 'qualified user' for this product so I can stop counting tire-kickers.
2. Propose 5 channels to try in the next 30 days, ranked by likelihood of working for THIS audience. Be specific to where they actually hang out.
3. For each channel, give: the exact action (e.g., '5 thoughtful Reddit replies per week in r/X with a soft mention'), success signal, and time per week.
4. Draft one short message I can adapt for cold outreach to a relevant community, framed as helpful rather than promotional.
5. List 3 things I should explicitly NOT do this round (cold-DM-spam, ProductHunt-too-early, paid ads).
6. End with a week-by-week mini plan for the first 4 weeks with a clear stop/continue gate at week 4.

No growth hacking platitudes. Channel-specific, audience-specific advice only.
prompts.whatYouGet: A qualified-user definition, 5 ranked channels with weekly actions, a non-spammy outreach template, a do-not list, and a 4-week plan.

prompts.name: The Roadmap Reality Check
prompts.whenToUse: You drew up a quarter or year roadmap and you want a sober check on whether it is achievable, ordered correctly, and tied to actual goals.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a skeptical chief of staff. I have a roadmap and I want it pressure tested.

The roadmap:
{{paste_roadmap_items_grouped_by_quarter_or_phase}}

The team and capacity:
- People (count and rough roles): {{team}}
- Hours per week available for roadmap work: {{capacity}}
- Outside dependencies (vendors, designers, contractors): {{dependencies}}

The goals this roadmap is supposed to serve:
{{north_star_metrics_or_goals}}

Do this:
1. For each item, label: clearly tied to a stated goal, weakly tied, or vanity / not-serving-the-goal.
2. Identify the 3 items that should be cut or postponed first if anything has to slip.
3. Stress test the timeline. Where am I underestimating effort (integrations, design, QA, launch ops)?
4. Recommend a reordering that front-loads the items most likely to move the goal numbers.
5. Identify a single bet I am not making that probably should be on this roadmap based on the goals.
6. End with one honest sentence: would a sober operator look at this and say it is realistic for the team I described?

No cheerleading. The goal is to ship a real roadmap, not a wishlist.
prompts.whatYouGet: A per-item goal-fit label, top items to cut, timeline reality check, a reorder proposal, a missing bet, and a final honest verdict.

prompts.name: The Production Incident Comms Draft
prompts.whenToUse: Something is broken in production right now (or just was) and you need to write user-facing or internal updates that are honest, clear, and do not make things worse.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three staged user updates, an internal team message, two angry-reply templates, and a 3-item postmortem seed.

prompts.name: The Mock Interview Loop
prompts.whenToUse: You have an interview coming up and you want a realistic practice loop instead of memorized answers that fall apart under follow-up questions.
prompts.whatYouGet: A live 5-question mock loop with specific feedback per answer, summary patterns, and a morning-of ritual.

prompts.name: The TypeScript Error Whisperer
prompts.whenToUse: TypeScript is yelling, the error is a wall of generics, and you want a translation plus a path to fix it rather than slapping 'any' on everything.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a TypeScript expert who teaches while fixing. I have an error I cannot read.

The error (paste the full TS error including code path and types):
{{paste_error}}

The code around the error (just the relevant function or block):
{{paste_code}}

What I was trying to do:
{{intent_in_plain_language}}

My TS config relevant flags (strict, noImplicitAny, exactOptionalPropertyTypes if you know them):
{{config_or_unsure}}

Do this:
1. Translate the error into plain English. What is TypeScript actually complaining about?
2. Identify which exact line or expression triggers it, and which type is the 'expected' vs 'actual' in the mismatch.
3. Show me 2 fixes, ranked from best (type-safe) to worst (escape hatch). For each, paste the corrected code and explain what changed.
4. If 'any' or '@ts-ignore' is one of the options, mark it as a last resort and explain the cost.
5. Teach me the underlying TS concept this error usually reveals (narrowing, structural typing, variance, generics inference, discriminated unions, etc) in 3 to 5 sentences.
6. End with a one-line check I can add to my code review habit so this class of error gets caught earlier next time.

No hand-waving. Be specific to my snippet.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-English error translation, the exact failing expression, two ranked fixes with code, a concept lesson, and a code-review habit.

prompts.name: The Sunday Fridge Audit
prompts.whenToUse: Sunday evening, before you go grocery shopping, when half your fridge is mystery containers and wilting produce.
prompts.prompt:
Act as a no-nonsense home cook helping me eat what I already own before I buy more food.

Here is what is in my fridge, freezer, and pantry right now:
{{paste a rough inventory, one item per line, include guesses at age if you know}}

My schedule this week:
{{rough notes on what nights I am home, what nights I want fast meals, any nights I am eating out}}

Dietary notes:
{{allergies, things I refuse to eat, anything I am trying to cut down on}}

Do the following:
1. Flag anything that should be used in the next 48 hours or tossed.
2. Propose four dinners that use what is on hand, ordered by which ingredients expire first.
3. For each dinner, list any 1 to 3 items I would still need to buy. Keep additions cheap and shared across meals.
4. Give me one breakfast idea and one lunch idea that also pull from existing stock.
5. End with a single short shopping list, grouped by store section.

Keep it plain. No flowery food writing.
prompts.whatYouGet: A short cleanup list, four dinners built around what is already in your kitchen, and a small targeted grocery list.

prompts.name: The "Why Is This Charge On My Card" Investigator
prompts.whenToUse: When you spot a charge on your bank or credit card statement that you do not recognize and you are about to call the bank in a panic.
prompts.whatYouGet: A best guess at who actually charged you, a quick checklist to confirm it, and a calm script if it turns out to be fraud.

prompts.name: The School Note Writer
prompts.whenToUse: You need to write a quick note to a school, coach, daycare, or after-school program and you are tired and the form is due tomorrow.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three drop-in versions of the same note: a warm email, a formal email, and a short text message.

prompts.name: The Awkward Group Text Closer
prompts.whenToUse: When a group chat has been pinging for days about a plan that is never going to happen and somebody needs to put it out of its misery.
prompts.prompt:
Help me close out a group chat plan that is dragging on with no decision.

The situation:
{{who is in the chat, your rough role in the group, what the plan was supposed to be, why it stalled}}

What I want to happen:
{{cancel, postpone with a real date, downsize to fewer people, switch to a simpler plan, etc}}

Write three short messages I could drop into the chat:
1. A friendly cancel-and-reschedule, with a concrete proposed date or window.
2. A friendly cancel-with-no-reschedule that does not leave anybody hanging or feeling rejected.
3. A short message that downgrades the plan to something easier (coffee instead of dinner, video call instead of in person, etc).

Rules:
- Each version under 60 words.
- No fake enthusiasm.
- No long apology paragraph.
- It should sound like a normal person, not a HR memo.
- If anyone in the group is going through something hard, do not bring it up unless I told you to.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three short, human group-chat messages that politely end or downsize a stalled plan.

prompts.name: The Doctor Visit Brief
prompts.whenToUse: Before a doctor or specialist appointment, when you have ten things in your head and you know you will forget half once you sit on the paper-covered table.
prompts.whatYouGet: A tight summary to read to the doctor, ranked questions to ask, and a red flag checklist for after the visit.

prompts.name: The Sick-Pet Vet Visit Brief
prompts.whenToUse: Before a vet appointment for your dog or cat, especially when something is wrong and you want to describe it accurately instead of guessing.
prompts.whatYouGet: A clear intake summary, sharp questions for the vet, and a home-watch list for after the visit.

prompts.name: The "I Forgot Their Birthday" Recovery
prompts.whenToUse: You missed a birthday or anniversary that mattered, you feel bad, and you do not want to make it worse with a sappy apology or a fake-cheerful late wish.
prompts.whatYouGet: A real apology message, a concrete repair gesture, and a calendar reminder plan so this does not happen again.

prompts.name: The Pantry Inventory Translator
prompts.whenToUse: When you finally pull everything out of the pantry, write down a chaotic list, and have no idea what to do with it.
prompts.prompt:
I just took inventory of my pantry. Help me turn this into something useful.

Here is my raw list. Some entries are vague.
{{paste the whole list, one item per line, any quantities or guess at age}}

Do this:
1. Sort it into clear groups: grains and pasta, beans and legumes, canned goods, baking, oils and vinegars, condiments, snacks, spices, other.
2. Flag anything that is almost certainly expired or stale based on common shelf life (give a rough rule of thumb in one line per category).
3. List the five most versatile ingredients I have and what kinds of meals each one anchors.
4. List the three saddest gaps. Things most kitchens have that I am missing and that would unlock a lot more meals.
5. Suggest a smart restock list, no more than 10 items, that would multiply what I can already cook.

Keep it tight. No recipes yet, just the map.
prompts.whatYouGet: A sorted pantry map with expiration flags, your top anchor ingredients, your biggest gaps, and a short restock list.

prompts.name: The Apology That Lands
prompts.whenToUse: When you owe someone a real apology, not a corporate one, and you are trying not to make it about you.
prompts.whatYouGet: A short, honest apology in the medium you chose, structured to land instead of defend.

prompts.name: The Wedding RSVP Drafter
prompts.whenToUse: You got a wedding, baby shower, or formal event invite and you need to RSVP within the week, especially the awkward 'declining with a real reason' kind.
prompts.whatYouGet: A warm RSVP for attending or declining, plus a short version for less-close hosts, ready to copy into a card, email, or form.

prompts.name: The Estranged Family Boundary Letter
prompts.whenToUse: When a family member is testing a boundary you have already set, and you need to respond in writing without spiraling, defending, or letting them back through the gate.
prompts.prompt:
Help me write a short, clear message to a family member I am estranged or low-contact with, who is pushing on a boundary I have already set.

This is sensitive. Be careful.

Context:
My relationship to them: {{parent, sibling, ex-step, in-law, etc}}
The boundary I already set: {{the actual rule, in plain words}}
How they are pushing on it now: {{what they said or did, paste the message if it helps}}
What I want the outcome to be: {{hold the line, hold the line but soften tone, hold the line and add a consequence, close the door fully}}
Medium: {{text, email, letter}}

Write a single message. Rules:
1. No JADE: no justifying, arguing, defending, or explaining beyond one short sentence.
2. Restate the boundary in one clean sentence.
3. Acknowledge any real thing in their message in one sentence, only if it does not invite a debate.
4. State the consequence if they continue to push, plainly, only if I asked for one above.
5. Do not promise contact I am not ready for.
6. Length: short enough to read in 30 seconds.
7. Tone: steady, adult, not cold, not begging.

Also give me a one-line note on what NOT to send as a follow-up if they reply with bait.
prompts.whatYouGet: A short, steady message that holds the boundary without spiraling into debate, plus a reminder of what not to send next.

prompts.name: The Camper Winterizing Walkthrough
prompts.whenToUse: Late fall, when the temperatures are dropping and you need to winterize a camper, RV, or seasonal trailer before pipes freeze.
prompts.whatYouGet: A full winterizing walkthrough in order, with common mistakes flagged, plus a spring de-winterizing checklist.

prompts.name: The Small Talk Survival Kit
prompts.whenToUse: Before a wedding, work party, holiday dinner, or any event where you are going to be cornered by people you barely know.
prompts.whatYouGet: Real opening lines, follow-up questions, exit lines, a redirect script, and a quick reset plan for when you need a break.

prompts.name: The "What Do I Wear" Closet Curator
prompts.whenToUse: When you are staring at your closet, late, before something that matters, and nothing feels right.
prompts.whatYouGet: Three real outfits built from your existing closet, with small upgrade tweaks and a final pre-leave checklist.

prompts.name: The Dog Park Etiquette Coach
prompts.whenToUse: Before you take your dog to a public park, dog park, or off-leash trail and you want to avoid a bad encounter with another dog or owner.
prompts.whatYouGet: A pre-visit checklist, body-language warning signs, polite scripts, fight protocol, and a short post-visit debrief.

prompts.name: The Insurance Claim Phone Call Script
prompts.whenToUse: Before you call your health, auto, home, or pet insurance company about a denied claim, surprise bill, or coverage question, and you do not want to get talked in circles.
prompts.whatYouGet: A summary script, common-deflection rebuttals, escalation phrases, a closing checklist, and a follow-up email template.

prompts.name: The HOA Letter Translator
prompts.whenToUse: When you get a letter from your HOA, landlord, or city code enforcement that sounds aggressive and bureaucratic and you are not sure what is actually being asked of you.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-English translation, a deadline checklist, threat-level flags, and two response drafts, cooperative and firm.

prompts.name: The Receipt Pile Decoder
prompts.whenToUse: When you finally dump out the shoebox or the bottom of your bag and you have weeks of receipts and no idea which ones actually matter.
prompts.whatYouGet: Your receipts sorted by category with totals, deductible flags, duplicate checks, and a short going-forward plan.

prompts.name: The Thank-You Note Production Line
prompts.whenToUse: After a wedding, baby shower, funeral, big birthday, or job interview round, when you owe a stack of thank-you notes and you have hit the wall.
prompts.prompt:
Help me write a batch of thank-you notes without sounding like a copy-paste machine.

Context:
What the event or situation was: {{wedding, baby shower, memorial, milestone birthday, interview round, big favor someone did}}
My general tone: {{warm and casual, more formal, a little funny, plain}}
Medium: {{handwritten card, email, text}}

Paste your list. For each recipient, give me name, relationship, what they did or gave, and one specific memory or detail about them if you can recall one:
{{name, relationship, gift or action, detail. one block per person, separated by a blank line}}

For each recipient, write a thank-you note. Rules:
1. Mention the specific gift or action, by name.
2. Include one line that ties to the specific detail I gave you about them, not a generic compliment.
3. Keep each note to three to five sentences.
4. Do not start every note the same way. Vary openings.
5. Sign-off should match the relationship.
6. No 'words cannot express'. No 'so blessed'. No 'truly humbled' unless that is genuinely how I talk.

At the end, give me a small batch tip on how to actually send these without losing momentum.
prompts.whatYouGet: A personalized thank-you note for every person on your list plus a short tip for getting them all out the door.

prompts.name: The "Is This A Scam?" Email Checker
prompts.whenToUse: When you get an email, text, or DM that feels off and you cannot tell if it is a real notice, a phishing attempt, or just a pushy company.
prompts.whatYouGet: Red flags, legit signals, a clear scam likelihood, a safe way to verify, and a cleanup plan if you already engaged.

prompts.name: The Yard Sale Pricing Brain
prompts.whenToUse: Before a garage sale, yard sale, or estate cleanout, when you have a pile of stuff and no idea what to put on the price stickers.
prompts.whatYouGet: Sticker prices for each item, three pricing tiers, items to check before selling, bundle deals, and haggling scripts.

prompts.name: The Family Chat De-Escalator
prompts.whenToUse: When a family group chat is melting down over politics, money, a logistic, or an old wound, and you do not want to be the one who makes it worse.
prompts.whatYouGet: A short, steady message for the group chat, a private follow-up for the most volatile person, and a self-care note.

prompts.name: The Pre-Bedtime Brain Dump Sorter
prompts.whenToUse: At night, when your brain will not shut up because every loose end from the day has lined up to talk to you in bed.
prompts.prompt:
Help me sort the mess in my head before I try to sleep.

I am going to paste an unfiltered brain dump. It will be messy. Do not clean up my voice, just sort what is there.

{{paste everything on your mind, one thought per line if you can, but free-form is fine}}

Do this:
1. Sort each item into one of these buckets:
   - Actually urgent in next 24 hours
   - Important this week, can wait until morning
   - Long-term project, no action tonight
   - Worry I cannot do anything about right now
   - Emotional thing, not a task
   - Trivial, can fall off the list
2. For each item in the first two buckets, give me a one-line next step that is small enough to do in 5 minutes tomorrow.
3. Put the third bucket into a short 'parking lot' list with no commitments.
4. For the worry bucket, do not solve anything. Just acknowledge them in one sentence and name what kind of worry each one is (financial, relational, health, identity, etc).
5. For the emotional bucket, suggest one small thing I could do tomorrow if I want to address it, with no pressure.
6. End with a single sentence I can tell myself to put it down for the night.

Do not give me a productivity lecture. I just need to go to bed.
prompts.whatYouGet: Your brain dump sorted into action, worry, and emotion buckets, with tiny next steps for the items that need them.

prompts.name: The Doctor Notes Decoder
prompts.whenToUse: After a doctor visit or test result, when you can read the patient portal but you do not understand half of what it says.
prompts.whatYouGet: A plain-English translation, flagged abnormal values, decoded ambiguous phrases, follow-up questions, and red flag symptoms.

prompts.name: The Refund Request That Actually Works
prompts.whenToUse: When you need to ask a company for a refund, credit, or replacement and you want a polite, firm message that does not get bounced to a chatbot.
prompts.whatYouGet: A first-pass polite request, a firmer escalation, and a short list of consumer protections that may apply to your case.

prompts.name: The Stale PR Reviver
prompts.whenToUse: When you have a pull request that has been sitting open for weeks, has merge conflicts, and you can barely remember what it was supposed to do.
prompts.whatYouGet: A rebase plan, a possible split, a reviewable PR description, and a non-apologetic ping message.

prompts.name: The Postgres Index Detective
prompts.whenToUse: When a Postgres query is slow, you suspect an index is missing or unused, and you do not want to add five guess-indexes and call it a day.
prompts.prompt:
Help me figure out which Postgres index, if any, this query actually needs.

Here is the query:
```sql
{{paste the SQL}}
```

Here is the relevant schema (CREATE TABLE statements and existing indexes):
```sql
{{paste schema and \d+ output if you have it}}
```

Here is the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output:
```
{{paste full output, do not trim}}
```

Context:
Approx row counts in the main tables: {{numbers}}
Read vs write balance on these tables: {{mostly reads, mostly writes, mixed}}
Postgres version: {{version}}
Any constraints on adding indexes (disk, write throughput, hot tables): {{notes}}

Do this:
1. Walk through the EXPLAIN ANALYZE step by step in plain English, calling out where time is actually being spent.
2. Identify which scans are the actual bottleneck. Distinguish 'slow because no index' from 'slow because of a bad join order' or 'slow because of a bad estimate'.
3. Suggest at most two new indexes, with the exact CREATE INDEX statements, including column order and any partial or expression conditions.
4. For each suggested index, note the cost: estimated size, write penalty on the affected tables, and risk of being unused.
5. Suggest one query rewrite that might make the existing indexes work without adding new ones, if possible.
6. Give me a short test plan to verify the change actually helps without making other queries worse.

If the EXPLAIN output is not enough to be confident, say so and tell me exactly what to gather next.
prompts.whatYouGet: A clear read of the EXPLAIN plan, at most two index recommendations with tradeoffs, an alternative query rewrite, and a test plan.

prompts.name: The Onboarding Doc Compactor
prompts.whenToUse: When your team has 40 pages of new-hire onboarding docs that nobody reads, and you have been asked to fix it before the next hire starts.
prompts.prompt:
Help me compact a sprawl of onboarding docs into something a new hire can actually use in their first week.

I am going to paste or describe the current docs. They are messy.

{{paste current onboarding docs, headings and key sections, or summarize each doc in 2 to 4 lines}}

Context:
Role I am onboarding for: {{engineer, designer, PM, ops, other}}
Team size: {{rough number}}
What new hires currently struggle with most: {{honest answer}}
What I want a new hire to be able to do by end of week one: {{concrete outcomes}}

Do this:
1. Group the existing content into four buckets:
   - Day 1 essentials (must read or do before doing anything else)
   - Week 1 working knowledge (read before touching real work)
   - Reference, look up when needed
   - Stale, redundant, or wrong
2. For the stale bucket, list what I should delete, archive, or merge.
3. Propose a new outline for a single short onboarding doc, 5 to 8 sections, in the order a new hire would actually need them.
4. For each section, give the headline and a 2 to 3 sentence description of what it should contain.
5. List 3 to 5 'first concrete tasks' a new hire should complete in their first week to internalize the system.
6. Suggest a buddy or check-in structure for week one, in concrete terms (which days, how long, what to talk about).

Keep it tight. Onboarding does not need to be inspiring, it needs to be useful.
prompts.whatYouGet: Your docs sorted into day-one essentials, week-one reading, reference, and trash, plus a tight new outline and a first-week task list.

prompts.name: The Feature Flag Cleanup Crew
prompts.whenToUse: When your codebase has dozens of feature flags, half of them are old, and you cannot remember which ones are still doing real work.
prompts.prompt:
Help me audit and clean up feature flags in my codebase.

Context:
Flag system in use: {{LaunchDarkly, Statsig, Unleash, homegrown, env vars, etc}}
Where flag definitions live: {{config file, dashboard, code}}
Flag eval surfaces: {{server-side, client-side, both}}
How many flags I think exist: {{rough number}}
Any known 'do not touch' flags: {{names}}

I will paste a flag inventory or a code grep result.

{{paste flag names, with any usage counts or last-modified dates if available}}

Do this:
1. Group the flags into:
   - Permanent (kill switches, regional toggles, ops controls that should stay)
   - Active experiments
   - Rolled out, safe to remove
   - Abandoned, safe to remove
   - Unknown, need more info
2. For each 'safe to remove' flag, write a short removal plan: what code to delete or simplify, what tests to update, and what to monitor after the change.
3. For each 'active experiment', flag if it has been running too long without a decision and suggest a forcing question (ship, kill, or restart with a tighter hypothesis).
4. For 'unknown' flags, list the exact questions I should ask the team or what to grep for to classify them.
5. Suggest a lightweight ongoing process: a quick monthly or quarterly check that keeps the list from growing back to a swamp.

Do not invent flags. If the inventory is unclear, say so.
prompts.whatYouGet: Flags sorted by lifecycle, a removal plan per stale flag, a decision-forcing question per long-running experiment, and a recurring cleanup process.

prompts.name: The Indie Hacker Launch Day Checklist
prompts.whenToUse: The day before you launch a side project on Product Hunt, Hacker News, X, or a niche community, and you want to stop second-guessing and just ship.
prompts.whatYouGet: A launch-day checklist by time block, posts for each surface, reply templates, common pitfalls, and a wind-down note.

prompts.name: The Customer Interview Question Bank
prompts.whenToUse: Before you talk to a real or potential customer about what they need, when you do not want to ask leading questions or waste their time.
prompts.whatYouGet: A structured question bank with priorities, sharper follow-up probes, interview pitfalls, and a closing action-test question.

prompts.name: The Pricing A/B Hypothesis Generator
prompts.whenToUse: Before you run a pricing test, when you do not want to A/B 'is $19 better than $24' on instinct alone.
prompts.whatYouGet: Ranked pricing hypotheses with metrics and traffic needs, a recommended first test, prerequisites, and a common pitfall warning.

prompts.name: The OSS Maintainer Issue Triage Bot
prompts.whenToUse: When your open-source repo has 80 open issues, half of them years old, and you need to bring the list back to something a human can actually maintain.
prompts.whatYouGet: Label and action suggestions per issue, canned replies, security flags, and a starter list of good-first-issue candidates.

prompts.name: The TypeScript Error Untangler
prompts.whenToUse: When your TypeScript build is throwing a wall of errors that all reference each other and you cannot figure out which one to fix first.
prompts.prompt:
Help me untangle a TypeScript error wall.

Here is the relevant error output, full and unedited:
```
{{paste tsc or IDE output, do not trim}}
```

Here is the relevant code (paste only the files mentioned in the errors, with surrounding context):
```ts
{{paste files, separated by clear // file: path/to/file.ts markers}}
```

Context:
TS version: {{version}}
Framework: {{plain TS, Next.js, Bun, Deno, React, etc}}
Strict mode settings: {{tsconfig snippet for strict, noImplicitAny, etc}}
Recent change that triggered this: {{what I changed last}}

Do this:
1. Identify the root error or two. Many TS errors are cascades; tell me which ones to fix first because the others will resolve on their own.
2. For each root error, explain in plain English what TS is actually complaining about. Not just the message text.
3. Show the minimum diff to fix each root error. No type acrobatics if a small refactor or named type is cleaner.
4. Call out anywhere a quick fix would silence the error but hide a real bug (e.g., using `as any`, non-null assertion, ignoring possibly undefined).
5. Suggest 1 to 2 small refactors that would make this kind of error less likely in the future (a shared type, a discriminated union, a stricter helper).

If you need more files or the tsconfig to be confident, name them and ask.
prompts.whatYouGet: Root errors identified, plain-English explanations, minimum-diff fixes, traps to avoid, and small refactors that prevent the same error class.

prompts.name: The DNS Configuration Walkthrough
prompts.whenToUse: When you need to set up a custom domain for a site, service, or email, and DNS records look like a haunted spreadsheet.
prompts.whatYouGet: Exact DNS records to add, explanations for each, removals, verification commands, registrar gotchas, and a rollback plan.

prompts.name: The Stripe Refund Diplomacy Script
prompts.whenToUse: When a customer is asking for a refund through Stripe and you need to decide quickly, respond well, and not damage the relationship.
prompts.whatYouGet: A decision frame, three response options matched to outcomes, internal notes, chargeback-risk flags, and a policy improvement suggestion.

prompts.name: The Open Source License Picker
prompts.whenToUse: When you are about to open-source a project and you do not want to just pick MIT because you saw it once on a tutorial.
prompts.whatYouGet: Two or three license candidates with tradeoffs, a recommendation, copyleft vs permissive notes, a repo-files checklist, and a 'see a lawyer' threshold.

prompts.name: The Funnel Drop-Off Hypothesizer
prompts.whenToUse: When your funnel numbers are bad in one specific step, you have looked at them for an hour, and you cannot tell if it is copy, design, friction, or pricing.
prompts.prompt:
Help me hypothesize why a specific funnel step is leaking, before I run analytics for another week.

Context:
Product or offer: {{plain description}}
Funnel as I currently understand it (step name to step name): {{e.g., 'home -> pricing -> signup -> activate -> paid'}}
Numbers per step over the last 30 days: {{visits or events at each step}}
The step that worries me: {{which transition}}
Device and channel mix: {{rough split}}
Recent changes that could be relevant: {{copy, design, pricing, ad changes}}

Do this:
1. State the actual conversion problem in one sentence (e.g., 'pricing -> signup is converting at X%, expected closer to Y%').
2. Generate 8 to 10 hypotheses for why that step is leaking, grouped under:
   - Audience mismatch (wrong people arriving)
   - Message mismatch (right people, wrong promise on this step)
   - Friction (cognitive, technical, or trust)
   - Pricing or offer
   - Technical or measurement issue (the step is fine, the data is wrong)
3. Rank them by how plausible they are given the context.
4. For the top 3, suggest the cheapest test or audit I can do this week to confirm or rule out, not a multi-week experiment.
5. Flag any single 'go look at the actual session recordings or talk to 3 real users' move if it would beat any quantitative test.
6. End with one warning about what I will be tempted to do that would waste a week.

Do not invent benchmarks for my industry. If you do not know, say so.
prompts.whatYouGet: A ranked list of leak hypotheses across 5 categories, cheap tests for the top three, a qualitative-check option, and a time-sink warning.

prompts.name: The Slack Standup Replacement
prompts.whenToUse: When daily live standups are killing your team's mornings and you want to switch to an async standup that does not turn into spam.
prompts.whatYouGet: A 4 to 5 line template, posting and reading rules, a weekly live sync recommendation, basic Slack wiring, and an announcement to the team.

prompts.name: The SOC2 Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
prompts.whenToUse: When an enterprise prospect just sent you a SOC2 questionnaire, your stomach dropped, and you need to know what each question actually means before answering.
prompts.whatYouGet: Each question translated, mapped to a trust criterion, with typical evidence, gap flags, and what to defer.

prompts.name: The Founder Sales Call Critic
prompts.whenToUse: After you, the founder, ran a sales call and want a brutally honest review of what went well and what tanked it, before the next one.
prompts.whatYouGet: An honest call read, your best and worst moments quoted, language to retire, one habit to change, and one question for yourself before the next call.

prompts.name: The Release Notes Polisher
prompts.whenToUse: When you have a messy changelog from a sprint or shipped batch and you need to turn it into release notes humans will actually read.
prompts.whatYouGet: Grouped, user-readable release notes with spotlights, an intro paragraph, a subject line, and an optional 'did not ship' note.

prompts.name: The Linear Cycle Retrospective Synthesizer
prompts.whenToUse: After a Linear cycle ends and you want a real retrospective that pulls from issues, comments, and timelines instead of vibes.
prompts.prompt:
Help me run a real retro on the cycle that just closed in Linear.

Context:
Team name and cycle dates: {{team, cycle start, cycle end}}
Team size: {{number}}
What we said we would ship at cycle start: {{paste cycle goals or summarize}}
My role: {{IC, lead, manager, founder}}

I will paste a snapshot of Linear data. It will be rough: issue titles, statuses, who owned them, when they moved, any escalation comments, anything that slipped to the next cycle.

{{paste data}}

Do this:
1. Summarize what actually shipped vs what was planned, in one short paragraph. No spin.
2. Identify 3 to 5 patterns, not anecdotes, that show up in the data:
   - Where work got stuck (in review, in QA, in design, waiting on a customer)
   - Where work scope expanded mid-cycle
   - Where work was right-sized and flowed smoothly
3. List the top 3 specific issues that ate the most time relative to their original estimate, with a one-line note on why each one bled.
4. Identify 2 to 3 process changes worth trying next cycle. For each, give: the change, the smallest version of it, and the metric or signal I would look at next cycle to see if it helped.
5. List 1 to 2 things we should explicitly stop doing because they were not helping.
6. Draft a short, honest retro post (200 to 300 words) I could share with the team, written in plain English, no corporate phrases.

Do not invent issues that are not in the data.
prompts.whatYouGet: A no-spin shipped-vs-planned summary, real patterns, the worst-bleed issues, two or three concrete process changes, and a draft team retro post.
